item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A well done apocalyptic prophecy scip. I was a bit leery about the GOC member escaping given the fact that the Foundation would almost certainly have kept such a subject under extremely high security until I thought of a reason for it. In my mind the Foundation deliberately let him escape and tell the tale to the GOC senior command and inso doing significantly reduced the chance that the GOC ever attempts to directly attack the Foundation. It just seems like the kind of sneaky, indirect scip usage the Foundation would utilise. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nuclear activities are mistaken as an enemy attack on the United States of America, resulting in a brief nuclear exchange between the United States and [REDACTED].

Kinda late, but I don't see the reason to redact this… :?

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded:
The 1st image in this article is a CC BY-SA image from https://www.flickr.com/photos/visitisrael/6091818631.


This automated message was sent on behalf of apoplexic. Please do not reply to this message or send other messages to this bot. Contact apoplexic in regard to this message. If you believe that you are not the intended recipient of this message, that the image was delivered incorrectly, or have other concerns about this bot, please contact anqxyr.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Super +1'd! Very well thought out, and I love the references to other scips. Makes total sense, and makes me more interested/invested in it. Though it would still work beautifully without the crossreferences. I actually gasped out loud when I read the 110-Montauk part (though that may just be because I have been on a 110-Montauk kick the last few days).
On a side note, I now tentatively headcanon that the reason why none of the prophecies come true is because its only *part* of the parchment scroll, and that if you read the entirety of the in-tact prophecy, the scenario that your mind conceives comes true. But hopefully the Foundation is able to pull through. 'Cos I like some hope in my grimdark :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only one problem with the current discription of the SCP; the prophicies would occur without Foundation intervention. I also understand that this would be counterproductive. +1 anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Makes me hope there are actual documents of XK-Class scenarios on the site. Like one in theory and with detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm of the opposite opinion, I hate it every time I see a blanked out cross reference. Either actually mention something else I can read about (preferably with link) or don't mention other SCPs.

With this one I agree that someone with knowledge of SCPs would assume one is involved in an XK

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was on the edge on this one. SCP-1050 reference tipped it, upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this very much; I agree with earlier comments that the 1050 reference is good, and the 231 reference is bad.

One other thing is bothering me:

Within 7-12 days of the date referenced in the text, the affected individual will come to believe that he or she is a "chosen one" who is personally responsible for preventing the event from occurring, and will attempt to engage in extreme measures to prevent the event, including political assassinations, displays of religious faith, human sacrifice, or sweatin to the oldies.

that redaction adds nothing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed; the person who is paranoid about terrorists has a vision of terrorists unleashing a biological weapon that kills the world; the person who's raised in a religious community has a Revelations revelation; the people who work with unknown eldritch THINGS see the ones they know about doing it all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. I wonder what would happen if it was exposed to someone/something that didn't care about the fate of the world, or actively wanted the world to end

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My brain is churning on the girl gone nuts in her cell, thinking everybody is a hallucination. The experiment log is definitely the most compelling part of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like it, but how is this an infohazard? Looks like a mind-affecting cognitohazard to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I'm not too keen on the 231 reference, I do like the 1050 reference and think it works.

This is good. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I daresay this is how to do SCP crossreferencing properly. Only two people with knowledge about the Foundation — one within and one without — had this effect happen; it makes sense. The wrong way to do it would have been "You read this and think SCPs will take over teh wurld!!1! Each person gets a different SCP."

And I mean, Smapti wrote this, of course he made it work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: whoops, how i shot reply lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They don't detract at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it complements the original that 110-Montauk not being performed on time is a greater threat than the nuclear war that precedes it.

This is what I mean. It serves less of a purpose as a plot device and acts more as "oooh 231."

when I asked myself what language-related SCPs could be part of an XK scenario, 1050 came to mind.

See, I think this could be done more effectively using SCP-████. Using someone else's SCP just because "it fits" doesn't work. I stand by what I said: the cross reference is not necessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly - it would be prety strange if a scroll that produces false XK predictions didn't mention any SCP-related apocalypses. In the case of the former I think it complements the original that 110-Montauk not being performed on time is a greater threat than the nuclear war that precedes it. In the case of the latter, I had come to the conclusion that the first Foundation researcher to examine the scroll would probably be a linguist, and when I asked myself what language-related SCPs could be part of an XK scenario, 1050 came to mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Strongly disagree, in this case. I'd be far more inclined to raise my eyebrows if someone who worked with (or against) the Foundation didn't immediately leap to an SCP when asked for the thing they thought would end the world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because we didn't realize at the time he'd come under the effects of an infohazard and it was believed that he was simply burning out and needed some time off duty.

Oh, okay, they hadn't figured out it was an infohazard until after he'd committed suicide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoting because this is great. But seriously, get rid of the unnecessary links to 231 and 1050. They add less than they detract.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
About the GOC field operative: did he already know all that stuff about the Foundation, or is the implication that the SCP someone leaked that knowledge to him?

Some of it was probably his personal observations from being in captivity, while the read he got from the scroll.

Why didn't they give him amnesiacs in an attempt to cure him? Did the Foundation think that seeing what would happen to a Foundation member with extensive knowledge about SCPs would be worth losing a valuable employee with lots of skills?

Because we didn't realize at the time he'd come under the effects of an infohazard and it was believed that he was simply burning out and needed some time off duty. Considering the potential side effects of amnesiacs, we probably don't dole them out like candy any time a senior researcher is feeling uneasy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On my first glimpse of the page image I thought "the Dead Sea Scrolls from Evangelion", which leads me to wonder if an anime fan would read an XK-class event involving giant robots piloted by emotionally screwed up teenagers.

About the GOC field operative: did he already know all that stuff about the Foundation, or is the implication that the SCP someone leaked that knowledge to him?

Dr. Parsons was a senior Foundation linguist exposed to SCP-1259 during initial study, before its anomalous effects became known.

Why didn't they give him amnesiacs in an attempt to cure him? Did the Foundation think that seeing what would happen to a Foundation member with extensive knowledge about SCPs would be worth losing a valuable employee with lots of skills?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: lawl.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Beautiful. I'm reminded of SCP-572.

Should "Global Occult Commission" be "Global Occult Coalition"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't have any strong feelings either way until I got to the experiment log. That made it interesting. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Smapti, you have been cursed. From now on, anytime you write a non-blackwood story, people will say "this is good, but more blackwood!"

You have been…

typecast!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You could probably lose "grammatically incorrect and consists entirely of" without changing the meaning at all, in fact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but I have a niggle:

"…analysis of these images, however, indicates that the photographed text is grammatically incorrect and consists entirely of a random arrangement of letters incomprehensible as a legible document."

Isn't the phrase "grammatically incorrect" a bit unnecessary?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So that explains Howard Camping and his stroke.

Seriously, nicely written. Upvoted.

Now write Lord Blackwood and the Chosen One!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems pretty mundane if not predictable, it's not terrible but it really doesn't do anything for me that any other 'prophecy' SCP already does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of the apocalypse book, that I keep forgetting the number of. But I still like it enough to give it a vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Go back to Lord Blackwood! :P
In all seriousness, I like this one. Well put together, interesting scenarios presented, and the reference to 1050 secured my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm taking a break from writing Lord Blackwood to present a twist on the old "The ancient prophecy says a chosen one will come to save us all" trope.
